title: Drop Coverage — Other Side, Same Pocket
game_context: 5-on-5 half-court. You are the point guard initiating a high ball-screen at the top of the key. Your screener (5) sets a clean screen on your LEFT this time. The screen defender (x5) is coached to drop on every ball-screen — he sits two steps below the level of the screen and protects the rim. Same coverage as DROP-01, mirrored to the left side.
possession_setup: Top-of-the-key high pick-and-roll. The screen comes from the LEFT. x5 is the read: he drops below the screen, chest below the level, weight back, feet pointing toward his own basket. The other three defenders are stationed in shell-rotation positions and do not stunt early.
decision_moment: You come off the screen toward the LEFT with one dribble of separation. x5 has not stepped up. The pocket between the screen and x5 is open. Your on-ball defender (x1) is trailing on your hip, two steps behind.
visible_cue: x5's chest is below the level of the screen — clear drop posture, same as on the right side. His feet point back toward the rim. The pocket between the ball-screen and x5 is wide open. Help defenders stay home.
prompt: Screen comes from the left. The big drops back. What's the read?

choices.label: Pull up in the pocket.
choices.feedback_text: Best read. Same pocket, other side — rise into the open shot before x5 can recover.

choices.label: Snake the dribble back to the middle.
choices.feedback_text: Okay. Snaking buys time, but the pocket pull-up was already there. You let x1 catch up.
choices.partial_feedback_text: A snake works against late shows, not against a settled drop. Read the chest — drop = pull-up first.

choices.label: Drive at x5's chest into the paint.
choices.feedback_text: Not yet. Driving into a drop big walks you into the contest. The space is in front of him, not past him — same as on the right side.

choices.label: Reset the ball to the wing.
choices.feedback_text: Not yet. Resetting wastes the pocket. Drop coverage is asking you to take the pull-up, regardless of which side the screen came from.

best_read: Rise into the pull-up in the pocket on the LEFT side. x5's posture is the green light — he's protecting the paint, not the screen, and there is no early help. The shot is open before the on-ball defender can recover. Same offer the defense made on the right side, just mirrored.
acceptable_reads: Snake back to the middle if you misread the depth — keeps the possession alive but trades the open shot for a contested re-read.
bad_reads: Drive into x5's chest — walks into the protected paint and a contest at the rim.
Reset to the wing — wastes a clean drop-coverage advantage.
common_miss_reason: Treating each side of the floor as a new puzzle. Players who learned DROP-01 on the right side sometimes drive into the big on the left because the geometry feels unfamiliar — but the cue is the chest, not the hand the screen was set with.
why_best_read_works: Drop coverage is built around protecting the rim on either side of the floor. The trade-off is the mid-range pocket — by definition the screen-defender cannot contest the pull-up without giving up his own assignment. The on-ball defender is trailing, so the only contest is x5 stepping up. He won't step up unless his coach changes the call. Mirroring the screen flips the strong side but not the read.
decoder_teaching_point: When the screen defender drops, the pocket is yours — left side or right side. Read his chest before you turn the corner: chest below the screen + feet to his rim = pull up.

feedback.correct: Good read. The drop gave you the pocket on the left, same as on the right.
feedback.partial: Snaking works, but the drop already gave you the shot. Trust the chest cue, both sides.
feedback.wrong: Look at his chest. Below the screen + feet back = drop. The side of the screen doesn't change the cue.

self_review_checklist: Did I read the chest before I committed, the same way on the left?
Did I take the pocket pull-up, or hesitate because it felt unfamiliar?
Did I attack toward open space, not into a body?
Did I trust that the cue grammar mirrors cleanly when the floor flips?

coach_validation.notes: Pack 2 (Phase δ-B mirror) — DROP-01 left-side mirror variant. Same single-freeze drop-coverage shape as DROP-01, mirrored across the y-axis. Mirror variants validate that drop-coverage recognition transfers across the floor; if pull-up choice rate drops vs DROP-01 the player has memorized geometry, not the chest cue. Inherits DROP-01 SME approval — no new cognitive surface.

source_research_basis: Mirror variants are the canonical post-mastery exposure pattern (see BDW-T1-02). Same source taxonomy as DROP-01: drop coverage as the universal entry-level PnR call.
explanation_md: **Read the Coverage — Drop, Other Side.** Same read as DROP-01, flipped to the left side of the floor. When the screen defender drops, he's telling you the call: protect the paint, give up the pull-up. The cue is his chest — if it stays below the level of the screen and his feet point back at his rim, the pocket between the screen and his body is yours. The trade is honest on either side: he won't contest the pull-up because that's not his job. **The side of the screen does not change the read.** If you took the pocket on the right, take it on the left.

wrongDemos.caption: You snaked back. The big stayed home and x1 caught up.
You drove into x5's chest. The contest was already waiting.
You reset to the wing. The pocket closed before the ball moved again.
